Indigenous Australians Civil Rights Movement← All Lessons
Lesson 2 of 4

The Freedom Rides & Wave Hill Walk-Off

💭 Thinking Question

If you saw someone being treated unfairly because of who they are, what would you do? What does it take to stand up for someone else's rights — especially when it's uncomfortable or unpopular?

🎯 Learning Intention

By the end of this lesson I will be able to explain the purpose and outcomes of the Freedom Rides in Australia, compare them with the American Freedom Rides using a Venn diagram, describe the Wave Hill Walk-Off and its significance, and analyse a song as a historical source.

⚠️ Warning: The video "Have you ever met an Aboriginal?" contains terminology no longer considered appropriate — this is noted below.

The Freedom Rides

Study the Freedom Rides information page from your booklet:

📹 Watch
Have you ever met an Aboriginal?

⚠️ Note: the term 'Aboriginie' used in this historical footage is no longer appropriate. Use 'Aboriginal' or 'First Nations Peoples'.

📹 Watch
Charles Perkins — Interview

The Freedom Rides Questions page from your booklet:

Freedom Rides Questions

1Based on the answers at the beginning of the video, how did the public feel about Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples?
2How much do you think most Australians knew about Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples at this time?
3In his interview, what sort of changes did Charles Perkins want to see? What actions was he calling for?
4According to Charles Perkins, what was the purpose of the Freedom Ride through rural NSW?
5What does Perkins mean by Aboriginal people being 'second class'?
6What do you think the living conditions were like in Aboriginal fringe settlements?
7Charles Perkins says the 'timing was right'. What do you think he means?

The Freedom Rides Cloze Passage

Study the cloze passage and word bank from your booklet, then fill in your answers in the text boxes below:

Cloze Passage — Fill in the Blanks

Use the word bank on the booklet page above. Type the correct word for each blank below.

1. Group name (SAFA full name)
2. Elected leader name
3. Man — his background/descent
4. Type of discrimination
5. Who hired the bus
6. Name of the trip/ride
7. What they surveyed locals about
8. Type of protest used
9. What was picketed in Moree
10. Where Aboriginal people had to sit separately
11. What Aboriginal people were from shops
12. What these restrictions are called
13. How opposition became
14. Media type 1
15. Media type 2
16. What Perkins could say at the end
17. What Australians voted at
18. How Australians voted
19. What the referendum supported
20. What Aboriginal people were counted in
21. Government's new power to do what

The Freedom Rides Comparison

Read the information about the Freedom Rides in America from your booklet, then complete the Venn Diagram activity below:

Venn Diagram — Australia vs America Freedom Rides

🌐 Both

The Wave Hill Walk-Off

Study the Wave Hill Walk-Off and lyric study pages from your booklet:

📹 Watch
From Little Things, Big Things Grow — The Wave Hill Walk-Off

Lyric Study — "From Little Things, Big Things Grow" by Paul Kelly

After watching the video and reading the lyrics in your booklet, answer the questions below:

Who was involved?
What caused the issue?
What was tried to resolve it?
What was the resolution?
Why is this event important in Australian history?

🪞 Lesson Reflection

What was the most significant moment or action you learned about in this lesson, and why?
Paul Kelly's song shows the power of persistence. Can you connect the message "from little things big things grow" to something happening in the world today?
The Freedom Riders were mostly young university students. What does this tell us about who can make a difference in society?
